title: Warning Stakes
desc: Yesterday's heat moved the safe line again, and the warning stakes need to move with it before somebody follows the wrong horizon.

steps.text:
No one bothers pretending the safe route through Mipedim Mirage is permanent. The heat shifts, the sight lines warp, and a path that made perfect sense yesterday can point straight into trouble by noon today. That is why the warning stakes are checked every morning: dark poles driven into the sand with weighted cloth markers that tell scouts and patrols where certainty ended most recently. This morning half the eastern line is wrong by three body lengths, which is not much until it is.

The route keeper sets down a bundle of fresh marked cloth. "Replace the faded warning flags if you want the easy reach," she says. "Or drive the new stake line along the corrected route if you would rather argue with the ground. Either way, we do not leave old instructions standing out here."

options.label: Replace the faded warning cloths on the old stake line
Drive the fresh stakes along the corrected route

steps.text:
The old cloths have gone pale with sun and sand until their signal marks blur at distance, which defeats the entire point of asking anyone to trust them. You untie each one in turn, shake the grit from the line loops, and knot on fresh dark markers whose stitched warning bars can be read even through a low shimmer. Once the first few are changed, the route looks decisive again instead of apologetic.

The keeper walks back along the line and checks visibility from three angles. Every flag catches cleanly now, snapping in the hot breeze like a set of concise arguments. "Better," she says. "A warning only works if it can be seen before the desert offers a prettier lie."

options.label: Gather the faded cloths and leave the line visible again

steps.text:
The corrected route lies across firmer ground than the old one, but the sand still resents being told where to hold still. You carry the stake bundle in stages, drive each post by mallet, and sight back to the ridge after every placement to keep the line straight. Heat already ripples low across the flats, creating little false offsets at the edge of vision, so you rely on the fixed markers rather than your first impression. That caution is the difference between a route and a rumor.

When the last stake goes in, the keeper walks the full line and checks the spacing with one hand on the guide cord. The posts read cleanly from ridge to flats, a thread of instruction laid across a field that would much rather confuse. "Good," she says. "Now the desert can change its mind without taking anyone with it."

options.label: Set the mallet down and leave the new route marked
